title: Evangelion going out of print
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion going out of print?             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon May 10, 2010 4:24 pm I'm placing this in the Everything Else forum, because this applies to the manga as well. So. It's 2010. Evangelion is still totally cool. But there's something to be concerned about... its availability. ADV Films imploded about a year ago, and the rights to Neon Gensis Evangelion and the Director's Cuts transferred to the rebranded Section23 Films, which is reportedly a totally different company, albeit composed of a lot of familiar names in a lot of familiar roles. But who's to say if they'll be reissuing Eva at any point in the foreseeable future, as various stores and outlets sell off the remaining sets. Manga entertainment no longer has the rights to Death & Rebirth or The End of Evangelion , despite their announcement in 2006 that they would be reissuing the films in a renewal/remastered format. These are starting to get scare. Considering the financial investment required to take on a title like this, there are only a few companies that could or would re-license the films. Funimation, get on that plox? The Evangelion Manga is similarly getting scarce, especially Volume 9, which seems to sell for high prices on eBay. Girlfriend of Steel 2, aka Angelic Days , was distributed by ADV Manga. Who knows if the rights for that are even held by one of the reformed ADV companies, or if they have any plans of re-issuing it. My guess would be no. This has me somewhat concerned. While there is always eBay and fansubs, the dwindling retail availability of Evangelion could start to negatively affect the Evangelion fandom. Thoughts?

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Mon May 10, 2010 7:16 pm It’s a valid concern. I know I’m not alone in hoping that NGE doesn’t turn into another ‘relic’ in the vein of Utena, whose DVDs are absurdly difficult to affordably obtain. I think NGE has a large enough audience over here to prevent it from staying OOP for too long, but given the nature of anime fandom on the whole, there’s no real guarantee anymore. I’m somewhat surprised Funimation still hasn’t obtained the Renewal EoE/Death stuff yet, TBH. EM wrote: The Evangelion Manga is similarly getting scarce, especially Volume 9, which seems to sell for high prices on eBay.

[]:             Quote Post by drinian » Mon May 10, 2010 7:22 pm Yep, it's not good that things are going out of print. However, new Evangelion fans are far more likely to encounter the show if it's available legitimately from one of the streaming video outlets like Hulu, Crunchyroll, Youtube, etc. DVD is dead for the casual user. Whether Section23 can make that happen is another question entirely. But, regarding DVDs -- I remember asking Matt Greenfield about the motivation behind the "Holiday Edition" Evangelion thinpack, and basically he said that retailers wanted new packaging to keep stocking a product. At this point, with A/V enthusiasts having moved on to Blu-Ray, I can't see there being a DVD reissue of the original Eva. The Blu-Ray market would be larger, since there would be people buying it again to replace their DVDs. Of course, the recent pattern seems to be that Blu-Rays will be released simultaneously for the Japanese and US markets, at similar prices, so who knows if any American licensor will be involved, beyond, perhaps, providing the dub track?

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Mon May 10, 2010 7:25 pm I think we're just meandering on the shit end of the economic sphere, and inevitably, as distribution companies tighten their belts, there's going to be a considerable period of time where releases and re-releases are going to be few and far between, but when/if the market regains stability, and the sale of animu and mango becomes lucrative again, then I don't see a reason why having all sorts of Eva goodies through US/Euro distributors will be too problematic. Evangelion in particular has a strong enough fanbase where people will devour merchandise, and GAINAX/ADV/Section23 knows this. I mean, why the hell would they releases a "Holiday Edition" while Platinum was still on the shelves if they didn't know that it would be scarfed up anyway. And if my prediction does fail, then it won't be that big of a deal to learn a little Japanese and import the stuff. A legitimate concern nonetheless.

[]:             Quote Post by AchtungAffen » Mon May 10, 2010 7:29 pm Why not the alternative of buying the thing from Japan and adding subs at home?

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon May 10, 2010 7:43 pm AchtungAffen wrote: Why not the alternative of buying the thing from Japan and adding subs at home?

[]:             Quote Post by drinian » Mon May 10, 2010 8:14 pm AchtungAffen wrote: Why not the alternative of buying the thing from Japan and adding subs at home?

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Mon May 10, 2010 9:29 pm I would not be surprised that in the next 10 years we see the day that nearly all media content produced will be downloaded over the internet the economics of hard copy DVDs/Blurays or books versus 100% digital downloads makes it clear where the profits will be if there are any to be had considering the expenses that the developed world is going to be forced to pay for just getting by in the coming years

[]:             Quote Post by Guyver Spawn » Mon May 10, 2010 10:31 pm Girlfriend of Steel 2, aka Angelic Days, was distributed by ADV Manga. Who knows if the rights for that are even held by one of the reformed ADV companies, or if they have any plans of re-issuing it. My guess would be no.

[]:             Quote Post by the_seventh_child » Tue May 11, 2010 10:10 am I am not really sure about everything else, but the NGE Sadamoto manga is pretty much rare to come across on 'official' sites. (early volumes especially) You can only find copies over at ebay and stuff. /especially volume 9, God, it is easier to find Lance of Longinus itself, rather than this. I was lucky to receive a copy from Carl himself otherwise..it was gonna be bai bai volume 9./

[]:             Quote Post by skikes » Tue May 11, 2010 12:23 pm Guyver Spawn wrote: I think they might soon, and I seen no point on re-dubbing it neither. I hope to see a re-release of End of Eva soon since the original DVD of the movie are find to find. I don't think NGE is going out of print neither.

[]:             Quote Post by Dr. Webster » Tue May 11, 2010 6:17 pm drinian wrote: However, new Evangelion fans are far more likely to encounter the show if it's available legitimately from one of the streaming video outlets like Hulu, Crunchyroll, Youtube, etc. DVD is dead for the casual user.

[]:             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Tue May 11, 2010 7:02 pm Sachi wrote: Because everybody can totally do that, right?

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Tue May 11, 2010 11:50 pm drinian wrote: A lot of Japanese Blu-Rays are coming with English subs these days.

[]:             Quote Post by drinian » Wed May 12, 2010 4:49 am BrikHaus wrote: Not Evangelion.

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Wed May 12, 2010 10:19 am If for some reason the LAEM project actually manages to lift off of the dirt, then you'd better believe store shelves will be neatly lined with reissues of the Evangelion anime and manga. Unfortunately, that's not looking too likely ATM unless something changes. I STILL don't have Volume 9 of the Evangelion manga and unless it goes back into print there's not much hope of me getting it. I wouldn't worry too much about the anime being OOP for the long term, though. It probably met market saturation a while ago, but eventually there will be enough demand for it to justify bringing it back into production. It's a popular and well liked series so it's only a matter of time before they put it out again, whether it's on another DVD set (which honestly I wouldn't need since I like Platinum just fine) or a further remastered Blu-Ray transfer.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed May 12, 2010 10:42 am drinian wrote: Gainax and George Lucas seem to have the same attitude towards releases on new media.

[]:             Quote Post by AchtungAffen » Wed May 12, 2010 4:52 pm Joseph the PRPD wrote: And because it's totally affordable.

[]:             Quote Post by drinian » Thu May 13, 2010 12:05 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: I didn't think Rebuild was made by Gainax. Either way, not even the Japanese Blu-Ray releases of Rebuild are equipped with English subs.

[]:             Quote Post by Fazmotron » Thu May 13, 2010 1:26 am drinian wrote: Since Japan and the US are in the same Blu-Ray region, this is actually plausible...
